---
title: Seal encryption and access
description: Notes are encrypted client-side, and decryption is gated by a Seal allowlist checked on every read. Revoking an agent denies its next session.
---

Notes never leave your device in the clear. Each note is encrypted client-side before it goes to Walrus, and who can decrypt it is governed by Seal, a threshold key-server scheme on Sui.

## Encryption

When a note is written, your client encrypts it against an identity tied to your vault. The ciphertext is also bound to the specific vault and note (additional authenticated data), so a blob from one vault cannot be replayed into another. The plaintext markdown and the keys exist only on your devices.

## How a decrypt is gated

To decrypt, a key holder needs the key servers to release their shares. The release is gated by an allowlist check called `seal_approve`. Here is the part worth understanding: `seal_approve` runs as a gas-free, off-chain dry-run. On every decrypt, your client builds the approval call and the key servers evaluate it as a dry-run against the current on-chain state. Nothing is submitted as a transaction, so checking access costs no gas and leaves no on-chain trace.

Because the check reads live chain state every time, the allowlist is authoritative the moment it changes.

## What revoking an agent does

Revoking an agent removes it from the vault's allowlist (a wallet-signed move, like all destructive actions). The effect:

- The agent's **next session** is denied. A fresh decrypt creates a new session key and re-consults the key servers, which now see the agent is off the allowlist and refuse to release shares. The agent gets a clean "no access" error, not a stack trace.

<Callout type="warn">
Revocation is not an instant universal lockout. A session key the agent already holds is valid until it ages out, and that window is short (the session key has a roughly 25-minute time-to-live). After it expires, the agent must start a fresh session, which is denied. Plan revocation knowing the next session is cut off, not necessarily an in-flight one.
</Callout>
